schools.summary: Matthew Henry laments the glory just departed and reads the boy-king's youth as the door to pride and to the seducers who undid Hezekiah's reform.
Clarke sketches the whole arc — 55 years, idolatry and murder, captivity, repentance, restoration, death at 67 — while Wesley notes the reign's reckoning includes the years in prison.
Gill dates the accession: Manasseh born three years after Hezekiah's recovery, in the seventeenth year of his father's reign.
Ellicott supplies the Assyrian and Egyptian background and glosses Hephzibah, 'my delight is in her,' a title of Zion in Isa. 62:4.

Keil and Delitzsch trace the corruption to a godless court party that captured the young king; Ellicott likewise blames the courtiers, comparing Rehoboam, and identifies the heathen as the peoples of Canaan.
Gill glosses the evil in a word: idolatry.
Clarke: Manasseh exactly copied the conduct of the nations God had cast out.

themes: a boy swayed by his court

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read a threefold gradation — rebuilt high places, Baal and Asherah after Ahab, and the new Assyrian host-of-heaven cult; Ellicott adds the philology of 'he returned and built' and the social history of the popular sanctuaries.
Clarke names the grove as Asherah (Mylitta/Venus) and the host as sun, moon, and stars; Wesley hears in 'he built' an act of contempt for Hezekiah's memory.
Gill: he rebuilt the temples and altars on the high places his father had destroyed.

Keil and Delitzsch stress the irony of altars in the house God claimed for his name; Ellicott reads the verse as the general statement particularised in v.5, echoing the promise to Solomon (1 Kings 14:21).
Gill places the altars in the holy place, distinct from the courts of the next verse.
Clarke: he placed idolatrous altars even in the temple.
Matthew Henry: Manasseh defied God to his face, desecrating the house and in effect turning God out of it.

themes: altars in the house of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott stresses that even the inner, more sacred court where sacrifice was offered was crowded with altars to the heavens; Keil and Delitzsch see the same relentless advance by degrees into both courts.
Gill: altars raised to the sun, moon, and stars.
Matthew Henry: the altars in the two courts were an affront offered under God's own eye.

Keil and Delitzsch place the child-sacrifice in the valley of Benhinnom after the manner of Ahaz and catalogue the soothsaying against the law; Ellicott notes the plural 'his sons' and the formal appointment of a court necromancer, with Moloch and the Queen of Heaven as new features.
Clarke unpacks each occult term — divination, enchantment, necromancy, wizardry; Wesley reads the fire-rite as dedicating the son to Molech in contempt of circumcision.
Gill: the son (Amon among others) was made to pass through the fire to Molech in the valley of Hinnom.

Keil and Delitzsch call the image in the Holy Place the very worst crime, against the LORD's word that the temple should house his name for ever; Ellicott names it the climax of impiety, comparing later cleansings and defilements.
Clarke insists Asherah must mean an idol, not a grove; Wesley alone takes the image to be of Baal worshipped in the grove.
Gill, following Selden, identifies the image as Asherah/Astarte, the Zidonian moon-goddess, and reports the traditions of a four-faced idol.

debates.question: What (or whom) did the graven image in the temple represent?

sides.position: an image of Asherah/Astarte, the moon-goddess
an image of the Baal worshipped in the grove

themes: the graven image in the Holy Place

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch root the pledge in 2 Samuel 7:10 and make its permanence conditional on obedience (1 Kings 9:6); Ellicott reads the un-wandering foot against the old migration to Egypt and adds the textual variants.
Gill: God's promise not to let Israel be carried captive rests on the condition that follows.
Clarke: had they been faithful they never would have gone into captivity, but would possess the land to this day.

themes: exile as the price of apostasy

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the deliberate breaking of the condition, Judah made worse than the Canaanites; Ellicott explains that Judah's guilt exceeded the heathen's because it forsook its own God for any foreign cult (Jer. 2:11).
Clarke: the king bent his whole power to corrupt the nation and succeeded; Wesley: Judah's greater light and new-invented idols made the same sins unspeakably worse.
Gill: they hearkened not to the voice of God in his law by Moses.
Matthew Henry: Manasseh both sinned himself and debauched the people, teaching and forcing them to sin.

themes: a king who seduced a nation

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch leave the prophets unnamed but reckon Habakkuk probably one (Hab. 1:5); Ellicott judges the phrase deliberately general and the oracle's style not Isaiah's, more likely his disciples'.
Matthew Henry reads the sending of the prophets as a graded mercy — teachers, then reprovers, then foretellers, then deputed judges.
Gill names three by the Jewish chronology: Joel, Nahum, and Habakkuk.
Clarke names five including Isaiah, and repeats that Isaiah was martyred by Manasseh, sawn asunder.

debates.question: Who were the prophets, and was Isaiah among them?

sides.position: Isaiah himself was among them and was martyred
not Isaiah himself but more probably his disciples; the style is not his
a named trio — Joel, Nahum, Habakkuk
unnamed, Habakkuk probably one of them

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read 'the Amorites' as a general name for the Canaanite races (instar omnium, as of Ahab's house in 1 Kings 21:26), and Ellicott removes the intrusive 'and.'
Gill refers 'these abominations' to the sins already catalogued.
Matthew Henry: Manasseh justified the Amorites by outdoing them and made a whole nation wicked.

themes: a king who made a nation sin

schools.summary: Gill: the covenant 'LORD God of Israel' is here the guarantee of Israel's doom, being just and sovereign as well as gracious.
Clarke hears in 'tingle' an onomatopoeic word for the ringing left by a shrill sound — the prophets' vehement testimony.
Ellicott reads the tingling ears as the shock of dreadful news (cf. 1 Samuel 3:11) and notes the 'her hearer' variant.
Matthew Henry: the judgment so terrible that the very report makes ears tingle and hearts tremble.

Keil and Delitzsch explain the line and plummet as tools of both building and levelling (Amos 7:7; Isa. 34:11); Ellicott reads the wiped, upturned dish as the destruction of people and city, or with Thenius a single 'making an end.'
Gill takes 'the line of Samaria' with the Targum as the line of destruction — the same lot as Samaria.
Clarke draws the full parallel with Samaria and Ahab's house and adds the Vulgate's blotted wax-tablet — Jerusalem the dish turned upside down 'to this hour.'
Matthew Henry: imitators fare as those they imitate; yet the dish is only wiped, not broken — emptied for purifying, not final destruction.

outliers.note: reads the wiped-but-unbroken dish as a sign of cleansing and future restoration, not the final ruin of Jerusalem.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the forsaking as the climax of the threat, but set beside it the Chronicler's sequel of Manasseh's own captivity, repentance, and restoration; Ellicott renders 'cast off' and cites the prey-and-spoil language (Isa. 42:22; Jer. 30:16).
Gill: only Judah with Benjamin is left of the inheritance, and now even that remnant is threatened.
Clarke: the ten tribes already forsaken, the remnant of Judah now about to be forsaken too.
Matthew Henry: justly are those forsaken who first forsake God — when he departs, their defence is gone.

themes: those who forsake God are forsaken

schools.summary: Ellicott sharpens the tense to a continual habit of provocation and prefers the LXX's 'since the day I brought forth their fathers'; Keil and Delitzsch treat this as the settled indictment behind the bloodshed of the next verse.
Gill glosses the evil once more as idolatry.
Matthew Henry: old guilt from the Exodus onward is called in, this generation reckoned with for treading in the fathers' steps.
Wesley: chiefly for Manasseh's generation, yet with respect to all their former sins.

Keil and Delitzsch cite Josephus on daily slaughter that filled Jerusalem with blood; Ellicott frames it (with Robertson Smith) as a bloody struggle between the reaction and the followers of the prophets.
Clarke paints Manasseh a merciless tyrant who slew people and prophets; Wesley identifies the blood as that of prophets and righteous men who reproved or refused him.
Gill takes the innocent blood to be the prophets and faithful put to death, Isaiah among them.

debates.question: Did Manasseh martyr Isaiah by having him sawn asunder?

sides.position: yes — received as fact/tradition, alluded to in Heb. 11:37
hardly probable, though the prophets were surely persecuted

themes: the tradition of Isaiah sawn asunder

schools.summary: Gill: the 'rest of his acts' were good and bad, for Manasseh repented and did many good things not recorded here.
Clarke supplies the Chronicler's account of captivity, repentance, and restoration, but doubts the apocryphal Prayer of Manasseh.
Ellicott reports that the best critics now allow 2 Chron. 33:11-19 to be genuine history, not edifying fiction.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read the garden of Uzza as the grounds of a summer palace Manasseh built for himself — a location, with no penitential meaning implied.
Wesley takes the garden-grave as a chosen testimony of repentance and self-abhorrence; Clarke reads it more plainly as the family burying-place, since Amon was buried there too.
Matthew Henry: Manasseh, truly humbled, judged himself unworthy of the kings' sepulchres — better to die repenting and be buried in a garden.
Gill states the bare fact: he died after a 55-year reign and a life of 67.

debates.question: Why was Manasseh buried in the garden of Uzza rather than the royal sepulchres?

sides.position: a deliberate token of sincere repentance and self-abasement
simply the garden of his own summer palace — a location, not a penitential act
the family burying-place, where Amon too would be laid

schools.summary: Gill dates Amon's birth to the forty-fifth year of his father's life, the thirty-third of his reign.
Matthew Henry sums the reign as short and inglorious and wonders whether Manasseh had sacrificed his elder sons to the idols.
Clarke cites the rabbinic pattern that sons of idolatrous kings seldom reigned more than two years (Nadab, Elah, Ahaziah, Amon).
Ellicott finds Egypt in the names — Amon the sun-god Amen — and glosses Meshullemeth and Jotbah.

outliers.note: conjectures that Manasseh's elder sons were sacrificed to his idols or refused by the people, since his heir was born only when he was forty-five.

themes: the two-year reigns of idolaters' sons

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch sum Amon's reign as lived wholly in his father's spirit — serving all his idols and forsaking the LORD.
Gill glosses the evil, again, as idolatry — the family sin passed down.

themes: imitating the sinner not the penitent

schools.summary: Gill reads 'all the ways' comprehensively — idolatry, witchcraft, and murders alike revived.
Wesley: Amon revived the idolatry Manasseh had put down, a warning that those who set bad examples cannot be sure the seduced will repent.
Keil and Delitzsch: a total, unqualified imitation of the father's way and idols.

themes: walking in all his father's ways
a father's repentance cannot unteach his sin

schools.summary: Ellicott expands 'forsook' into a total abandonment for foreign superstition and surveys the whole Manasseh-Amon epoch through Zephaniah's rebellious city; Keil and Delitzsch echo the bare charge of desertion.
Gill hears 'the God of his fathers' as the God of David and Solomon, deliberately abandoned.

themes: forsaking the God of his fathers
the reign seen only through religion

schools.summary: Ellicott takes 'servants' as courtiers or palace officials and confesses the circumstances unknown; Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative on to the people's vengeance and Josiah's accession.
Gill supposes a private grievance — 'some ill usage' — not idolatry, as the motive.
Clarke confesses the reason unknown, but notes the deed was unpopular and would be avenged.
Matthew Henry hears divine justice beneath the crime: he who profaned God's house with idols had his own house polluted with his blood.

debates.question: Why did Amon's servants conspire against him?

sides.position: a personal grievance or ill usage, not his idolatry
the cause is simply unknown; the act was unpopular

themes: his own house polluted with blood

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take 'the people of the land' as the general population of Judah (cf. 2 Chron. 26:1) who crowned the eight-year-old Josiah; Ellicott rejects Thenius's narrower 'militia.'
Gill reads the rising as vengeance for a king unlawfully slain.
Matthew Henry: the people did justice on the traitors and a kindness to themselves in crowning Josiah — a happy change and 'once more tried with a reformation.'

outliers.note: took 'the people of the land' to be the militia (as at 2 Kings 11:14); Ellicott judges it unlikely.

schools.summary: Gill passes over the formula with a bare cross-reference — the same closing notice already explained, but now holding no record of good.
Clarke's earlier verdict frames the emptiness: Amon was as bad as his father at first but 'did not copy his father's repentance.'

themes: a reign too short and wicked for record

schools.summary: Clarke turns the garden-burial into a long proof that Asherah everywhere means an idol, not a grove — marshalling Jarchi, Castel, Theodoret, Josiah's reforms, and the versions.
Gill: Amon laid in the garden of Uzza, where his father Manasseh was buried.
Ellicott: the tomb was prepared near his father's, and the chapter closes on the accession of Josiah.

themes: Asherah an idol, not a grove
